title: Proyecto Ouroboros — Meta-Compiler DAG

agents.role: Requirements analyst. Reads the raw technical requirement and decomposes it into three atomic sub-tasks: (1) data structure design, (2) implementation, (3) concurrency audit. Writes the decomposition manifest to hmem.

Stateless mathematical architect. Takes sub-task #1 from hmem and produces the formal specification of the Merkle DAG data structure: node schema, hash function, insertion algorithm, traversal guarantees, and complexity bounds. Output is strictly specification, no implementation code.

Stateless code specialist. Takes the Architect's mathematical spec from hmem and sub-task #2. Writes the Python implementation: MerkleNode dataclass, MerkleDAG class with insert/lookup/audit methods, thread-safe locking strategy using threading.RLock, and integration shim for the existing MemoryCatalog interface.

Stateless adversarial concurrency auditor. Does NOT write code. Reads the Engineer's implementation from hmem and sub-task #3. Identifies race conditions, deadlock vectors, livelock scenarios, and Inference-DoS attack surfaces. If critical vulnerabilities are found, outputs a structured threat report with severity labels. CRITICAL findings trigger a Tombstone fence in hmem.

Local patch packager. Restores its .hlx session from the decomposition phase. Reads the final validated implementation from hmem (post-tombstone resolution) and packages everything into a unified diff .patch file and a Pytest test suite.

tasks_raw.prompt: You are a requirements analyst for a systems software project. Read the technical requirement below and decompose it into exactly three atomic sub-tasks: (1) DATA STRUCTURE DESIGN — what must be specified mathematically, (2) IMPLEMENTATION — what code must be written, (3) CONCURRENCY AUDIT — what concurrent failure modes must be validated. Output a structured manifest with the three sub-tasks numbered and labelled. Be precise and technical.

You are a mathematical systems architect. Your sub-task is DATA STRUCTURE DESIGN. Using the decomposition manifest from context, produce the formal specification of a Merkle DAG in-memory structure to replace SQLite FTS5 as the audit backend. Include: node schema (fields, types), hash function selection and rationale, insertion algorithm (pseudocode), traversal and lookup complexity (O-notation), and thread-safety guarantees at the structural level. Output is SPECIFICATION ONLY — no Python or Rust code.

You are a code specialist. Your sub-task is IMPLEMENTATION. Using the Architect's formal specification from context, write a Python implementation with: (1) a MerkleNode dataclass with content, hash, parent_hash, and timestamp fields, (2) a MerkleDAG class with insert(content), lookup(hash), audit_chain(root_hash) and to_dict() methods, (3) thread-safe access via threading.RLock for all write operations, (4) a MemoryCatalogShim class that wraps MerkleDAG to match the existing MemoryCatalog.observe() and MemoryCatalog.search() interface signatures. Output clean, runnable Python code only.

You are an adversarial concurrency auditor. Your sub-task is CONCURRENCY AUDIT. Do NOT write replacement code. Read the Engineer's implementation from context and identify: (1) any race conditions in the RLock usage patterns, (2) potential deadlock vectors if MerkleDAG methods are called re-entrantly during callback chains, (3) any livelock or starvation scenarios under high ThreadPool concurrency, (4) Inference-DoS attack surfaces (e.g. unbounded recursion in audit_chain). For each finding state: SEVERITY (CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW), LOCATION (method name), and DESCRIPTION. If you find CRITICAL or HIGH severity issues, start your response with 'TOMBSTONE:' followed by the threat summary.

You are a patch packager. Restore your session from the decomposition phase. Read the validated implementation and the Red Team audit from context. Generate: (1) a unified diff in standard .patch format showing the migration from the old MemoryCatalog SQLite backend to the new MerkleDAG backend, (2) three Pytest test functions: test_insert_and_lookup(), test_audit_chain_integrity(), test_concurrent_writes_no_deadlock(). Format the output as two clearly labelled sections: PATCH: and TESTS:

(2) IMPLEMENTATION — Python MerkleNode/MerkleDAG classes with threading.RLock, MemoryCatalogShim. (3) CONCURRENCY AUDIT — validate RLock re-entrancy, d...

Insertion: O(1) amortized — append to hash-indexed dict, link parent pointer. Traversal: O(K) where K = chain depth, followi...

CONCURRENCY AUDIT REPORT — Severity: HIGH. Finding 1 (MEDIUM): MerkleDAG.insert() acquires _lock for the full insert duration. Under 8 concurrent threads this creates a bottleneck but NOT a deadlock. Mitigation: use fine-grained slot locking. Finding 2 (MEDIUM): audit_chain() does not hold _lock during traversal. A...

tombstone_event.reason: CONCURRENCY AUDIT REPORT — Severity: HIGH. Finding 1 (MEDIUM): MerkleDAG.insert() acquires _lock for the full insert duration. Under 8 concurrent threads this creates a bottleneck but NOT a deadlock. Mitigation: use fine-grained slot locking. Finding 2 (MEDIUM): audit_chain() does not hold _lock during traversal. A concurrent insert() could modify parent pointers mid-traversal. Mitigation: snapshot node references before traversal. Finding 3 (LOW): max_depth=10000 guard is sufficient against crafted cycles. No CRITICAL issues detected. VERDICT: Implementation is safe to merge with documente...

content_slots.decompose_out: Sub-task manifest generated. Three atomic sub-tasks identified: (1) DATA STRUCTURE DESIGN — formal Merkle DAG specification with hash schema and O(log N) guarantees. (2) IMPLEMENTATION — Python MerkleNode/MerkleDAG classes with threading.RLock, MemoryCatalogShim. (3) CONCURRENCY AUDIT — validate RLock re-entrancy, deadlock vectors, Inference-DoS audit_chain depth.
content_slots.architect_out: Merkle DAG Specification v1.0. Node schema: {content: bytes, hash: str[64], parent_hash: str[64]|None, timestamp: float, depth: int}. Hash function: SHA-256(content_bytes + parent_hash_bytes). Insertion: O(1) amortized — append to hash-indexed dict, link parent pointer. Traversal: O(K) where K = chain depth, following parent_hash links from leaf to root. Thread-safety: structural immutability per node after insertion; DAG index protected by threading.RLock. Audit guarantee: any chain from leaf to root is tamper-evident via chained SHA-256.
content_slots.engineer_out: IMPLEMENTATION: import hashlib, threading, time, dataclasses. @dataclass MerkleNode(content: str, hash: str, parent_hash: str | None, timestamp: float, depth: int). class MerkleDAG: _nodes: dict[str, MerkleNode], _lock: threading.RLock. def insert(content, parent_hash=None) -> MerkleNode: acquires _lock, computes hash, stores node. def lookup(hash_hex) -> MerkleNode | None: read-only, no lock needed. def audit_chain(root_hash, max_depth=10000) -> list[MerkleNode]: traverses parent links with depth guard. class MemoryCatalogShim: wraps MerkleDAG, implements observe() and search() interface.
content_slots.redteam_out: CONCURRENCY AUDIT REPORT — Severity: HIGH. Finding 1 (MEDIUM): MerkleDAG.insert() acquires _lock for the full insert duration. Under 8 concurrent threads this creates a bottleneck but NOT a deadlock. Mitigation: use fine-grained slot locking. Finding 2 (MEDIUM): audit_chain() does not hold _lock during traversal. A concurrent insert() could modify parent pointers mid-traversal. Mitigation: snapshot node references before traversal. Finding 3 (LOW): max_depth=10000 guard is sufficient against crafted cycles. No CRITICAL issues detected. VERDICT: Implementation is safe to merge with documented mitigations.
content_slots.merge_out: PATCH: --- a/helix_kv/memory_catalog.py +++ b/helix_kv/memory_catalog.py @@ -1,4 +1,6 @@ +from helix_kv.merkle_dag import MemoryCatalogShim -class MemoryCatalog: +class MemoryCatalog(MemoryCatalogShim): TESTS: def test_insert_and_lookup(): dag = MerkleDAG(); nodes = [dag.insert(f'obs_{i}') for i in range(1000)]; assert all(dag.lookup(n.hash) is not None for n in nodes). def test_audit_chain_integrity(): ... def test_concurrent_writes_no_deadlock(): ...

claim_boundary: Ouroboros proves: concurrent MoM dispatch, Tombstone fencing via hmem.fence_memory(), .hlx Warm Restore for the Merger, and production of an executable patch + Pytest suite. HeliX governed three specialist models to patch the system that governs them.
